summary: This notebook implements a segmentation pipeline for scientific image forgery detection using a hybrid architecture that combines a frozen DINOv2 visual encoder with a lightweight CNN decoder. It processes paired forged images with binary masks and authentic images with zero masks, training only the decoder head with BCEWithLogitsLoss. During inference, it applies gradient-enhanced adaptive thresholding and morphological refinement to generate precise forgery masks, followed by area and mean-probability filters to classify images as authentic or forged. The solution demonstrates how self-supervised embeddings can be efficiently adapted for pixel-level manipulation localization without full model fine-tuning.
competition_flow: Loads authentic and forged scientific images with corresponding binary masks, trains a frozen DINOv2 encoder with a trainable CNN decoder using a 20% holdout split, and generates pixel-level segmentation masks with adaptive post-processing for a Kaggle submission.
data_reading: Reads image paths from /train_images/authentic and /train_images/forged directories, loads .npy binary masks from /train_masks, and handles test images from /test_images.
Masks are loaded via np.load, converted to binary with (m > 0).astype(np.uint8), and resized using cv2.resize.
data_processing: Resizes all images and masks to 256×256, normalizes pixel values to [0,1], and applies cv2.INTER_NEAREST for mask resizing.
Inference includes Sobel gradient extraction, Gaussian blurring, morphological closing/opening, and dynamic thresholding (mean + 0.3*std).
insights: Freezing a self-supervised ViT encoder while training only a lightweight CNN decoder reduces memory usage and training time without sacrificing segmentation quality.
Gradient-aware adaptive thresholding combined with morphological operations produces sharper and more accurate forgery masks than raw model outputs.
Filtering predictions by detected area and mean probability inside the mask effectively reduces false positives on authentic images.
